title: Sunlight Exposure, Vitamin D & Skin Health
source: NIH ODS, WHO, Dermatology Research, Endocrine Society

chunks.title: Recommended Sunlight Duration for Vitamin D Synthesis
chunks.content:
NIH Office of Dietary Supplements guidelines for vitamin D via sun:
- 10-30 minutes of midday sun (10 AM - 3 PM) on bare skin several times per week is usually adequate for lighter-skinned individuals.
- Expose face, arms, and legs without sunscreen during this brief window.
- After 10-30 min, apply sunscreen SPF 30+ to prevent skin damage.
- Best time: Solar noon ± 2 hours when UVB is strongest.
- Frequency: 2-3 times per week minimum.
Vitamin D production drops dramatically outside the 10 AM-3 PM window because UVB is filtered by the atmosphere at low sun angles. Source: NIH ODS, Holick MF (2007).

chunks.title: Sunlight Duration by Skin Type (Fitzpatrick Scale)
chunks.content:
Melanin absorbs UVB, so darker skin needs more sun exposure for equivalent vitamin D:
Fitzpatrick Skin Type I (very fair, always burns): 10-15 min midday
Fitzpatrick Skin Type II (fair, burns easily): 15-20 min midday
Fitzpatrick Skin Type III (medium, sometimes burns): 20-30 min midday
Fitzpatrick Skin Type IV (olive, rarely burns): 30-40 min midday
Fitzpatrick Skin Type V (brown, very rarely burns): 40-60 min midday
Fitzpatrick Skin Type VI (dark brown/black, never burns): 60-80 min midday
These are approximate for summer, midday, at ~35° latitude (e.g., Los Angeles, New Delhi, Cairo). Multiply by 1.5-2x in winter or at higher latitudes. Source: Webb & Holick (1988), genexdiagnostics.com.

chunks.title: Vitamin D Synthesis by Latitude and Season
chunks.content:
Latitude strongly affects UVB availability:
- Equator to 25° latitude: Year-round adequate UVB for vitamin D (3-15 min midday for light/dark skin).
- 25-40° latitude (e.g., US South, India, Mediterranean): Adequate most of the year, limited Dec-Feb.
- 40-50° latitude (e.g., New York, London, Beijing): 'Vitamin D winter' from Nov-Feb — little to no vitamin D synthesis even with prolonged exposure.
- >50° latitude (e.g., Scotland, Scandinavia, Canada): Vitamin D winter from Oct-Mar — supplementation recommended.
Additional factors: Cloud cover reduces UVB ~50%. Air pollution reduces UVB 10-60%. Glass blocks virtually all UVB. Altitude: UVB increases ~10-12% per 1000m elevation. Source: NIH ODS, Webb et al. (1988), Engelsen (2010).

chunks.title: Vitamin D Blood Levels and Supplementation
chunks.content:
Serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D [25(OH)D] levels (NIH/Endocrine Society):
- Deficient: < 30 nmol/L (12 ng/mL) — risk of rickets in children, osteomalacia in adults.
- Insufficient: 30-49 nmol/L (12-19 ng/mL) — suboptimal bone health.
- Sufficient: 50-125 nmol/L (20-50 ng/mL) — adequate for most people.
- High: > 125 nmol/L (50 ng/mL) — potential adverse effects.
RDA for vitamin D supplementation (when sun is insufficient):
- Infants (0-12 months): 400 IU/day
- Children/Adults (1-70 years): 600 IU/day
- Older adults (71+ years): 800 IU/day
- Pregnant/breastfeeding: 600 IU/day
- The Endocrine Society suggests higher amounts (1500-2000 IU/day) for adults at risk of deficiency. Source: NIH ODS, IOM DRI (2011), Endocrine Society Guidelines.
